2005.assessment: GOSS newly established. CPA oil revenues beginning. GOSS budget largely funded by oil transfers from Khartoum under CPA. Minimal debt at GOSS level. But dependence on single revenue source extreme. Technically low debt (no sovereign debt) but extreme oil-revenue dependency. Functionally, manageable in the sense of surplus/low-debt as long as oil flowed.

2006.assessment: Oil revenues flowing. GOSS budget in surplus from oil. No external debt at GOSS level. Spending on reconstruction and security. Corrupt leakage of revenues. Technically surplus/low debt.

2007.assessment: GOSS oil revenues at peak. Budget surplus. Massive corruption ($4B+ estimated embezzled over CPA period). No formal external debt. Surplus.

2008.assessment: Oil price windfall 2008. GOSS budget in strong surplus. No external debt. Peak fiscal position.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil price decline. GOSS revenue fell significantly. From surplus to possible deficit. No accumulated savings reserve (oil revenues spent). Small deficit or near-balanced depending on spending.

2010.assessment: Oil recovering. Pre-referendum elections funded by government. GOSS budget approximately balanced to slight deficit. No external debt.

2011.assessment: Independence. South Sudan inherited Sudan's legacy debt allocation (negligible). IMF provided initial support. Oil revenues strong. Government in surplus at independence. But 98%+ oil revenue dependency means extreme fragility. Low debt but structurally unsustainable.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 eliminated ~98% of government revenue. Government began emergency borrowing to pay salaries. Fiscal collapse. South Sudan drew on emergency reserves and borrowing. Unsustainable debt crisis conditions despite low absolute debt.

sources.citation: IMF: South Sudan Article IV 2012 — oil shutdown

2013.assessment: Oil resumed — fiscal position recovering. Debt from 2012 emergency borrowing being repaid. Small deficit to near balanced in 2013 before December civil war. Civil war December began new fiscal crisis. Manageable first three quarters.

2014.assessment: Civil war. Unity State oil production disrupted. Military spending surged. Global oil prices fell. Large deficit emerging. IMF program discussions began. Unsustainable debt crisis developing.

2015.assessment: Unity oil fields destroyed. Global oil crash. Both oil revenue and production down. Government accumulated massive arrears (unpaid salaries, vendor payments). Unsustainable debt crisis — fiscal collapse conditions.

2016.assessment: Fiscal collapse. Central Bank printing money creating hyperinflation. Government unable to service debt or pay salaries. Default on external obligations. IMF described South Sudan as in fiscal collapse. Debt-to-GDP meaningful but irrelevant given no fiscal function.

sources.citation: IMF: South Sudan Article IV 2016 — fiscal collapse

2017.assessment: Famine declared. Fiscal collapse continuing. International aid keeping basic government function. Oil revenues minimal. Debt accumulating. Fiscal collapse conditions.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Some oil production recovery. Fiscal stabilization beginning. Debt accumulated during crisis. Unsustainable debt crisis — debt/GDP likely very high but oil revenue improving.

2019.assessment: Oil revenues recovering with production improvement. Fiscal conditions improving from collapse. Still large debts/arrears. IMF monitoring. Unsustainable but improving trend.

2020.assessment: COVID + oil price crash 2020. Revenue collapsed again. Fiscal position reversed from improving. Government again unable to pay salaries consistently. Large deficit. Unsustainable.

2021.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Revenue improved. IMF monitoring. Large accumulated arrears still outstanding. Large deficit/high debt. Improving but still large deficit high debt condition.

2022.assessment: High oil prices (Ukraine war effect). Best fiscal conditions since civil war. Government revenue strong. Arrears being reduced. Large deficit/high debt situation improving.

2023.assessment: Sudan war April 2023 disrupted pipeline. Oil export revenues severely impacted. Fiscal position deteriorated sharply. Government revenue crisis returning. Unsustainable debt crisis conditions re-emerging.

2024.assessment: Sudan war continued disrupting oil exports. Machar detained — political instability. Fiscal collapse conditions. Government unable to fund basic services. Civil service salaries unpaid. Fiscal collapse.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, oil disruption, political instability. Fiscal collapse conditions continuing.

2005.assessment: Pre-independence southern Sudan. GDP per capita extremely low — near bottom globally. CPA oil revenues beginning to flow to GOSS but most population subsistence farmers and herders. World Bank does not have formal GDP data for this period. Estimated $300-500/capita based on CPA wealth sharing flows and population estimates. Near peak of CPA oil period.

sources.citation: GOSS Budget Data 2005; CPA Wealth-Sharing Implementation

2006.assessment: Oil revenues to GOSS growing. CPA wealth-sharing transfers to Juba. GDP per capita growing from very low base as oil money circulated in Juba economy. Still near bottom globally. Estimated $400-600/capita. Near peak for CPA period, improving.

2007.assessment: Oil revenues at peak for GOSS pre-secession. Juba construction boom. GOSS budget over $1 billion. Population still overwhelmingly rural subsistence. Per capita nationally very low despite oil-funded Juba. Estimated $600-800/capita if distributed; actual subsistence living for most. Near peak for CPA period.

2008.assessment: Oil price spike 2008 created windfall. GOSS revenues surged. Global financial crisis later in year. Per capita national figures low but oil revenues highest in pre-independence period. Near peak or above for CPA oil era.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil price decline reduced GOSS revenues. Economic contraction from GOSS perspective. Population still subsistence. Moderate decline from 2008 peak. Significant decline from 2008 level.

2010.assessment: Oil revenues recovering with rising prices. Pre-referendum period. Elections held. GOSS revenues improving. 2010 elections funded partly through oil. Near peak for CPA period.

2011.assessment: Independence year — World Bank began formal tracking. $1,111 per capita was peak for South Sudan. Independence excitement, international recognition, aid inflows, oil revenues all contributed. This became the reference peak for subsequent years.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2011 = $1,111

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 — all oil production halted for nearly a year. Massive revenue loss. GDP contracted sharply. ~$946 per capita vs $1,111 peak — significant decline (~85% of peak). Significant decline from 2011 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2012 ~$946

2013.assessment: Oil production resumed late 2012 and through 2013. Recovery partial. ~$1,085 per capita — near peak levels. But December 2013 civil war outbreak began economic collapse. Year average near peak but trajectory turning.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2013 ~$1,085

2014.assessment: Civil war through 2014. Oil production disrupted in Unity State (conflict zone). GDP per capita ~$993 — significant decline from $1,111 peak (~89%). Global oil prices also fell. Significant decline from peak.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2014 ~$993

2015.assessment: Civil war continued. Oil prices crashed globally. Unity State oil fields destroyed by SPLA-IO attacks. GDP collapsed to ~$663 per capita — 60% of peak. Severe decline.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2015 ~$663

2016.assessment: 2016 was catastrophic economic year. ARCSS collapse July 2016. Currency collapse — South Sudan pound lost 800%+ value. Hyperinflation. GDP per capita collapsed to ~$237 — only 21% of $1,111 peak. Near economic collapse.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2016 ~$237

2017.assessment: Famine declared in Unity State. Hyperinflation continuing. Slight nominal GDP recovery in USD terms (~$293) but still collapse. Oil production minimal. ~26% of peak. Near collapse.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2017 ~$293

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS signed September 2018. Some economic stabilization. Oil production slowly recovering. GDP per capita ~$359 — 32% of peak. Still collapse-level but slight improvement from 2016 bottom.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2018 ~$359

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS improvement. Oil production continuing to recover. GDP per capita ~$398 — 36% of peak. Still severe decline but trajectory improving.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2019 ~$398

2020.assessment: COVID-19 + oil price crash 2020. Unity government formed Feb 2020 but COVID disrupted economic activity. Oil prices crashed. GDP per capita fell to ~$318 — 29% of peak. Near economic collapse again.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2020 ~$318

2021.assessment: Oil prices recovering 2021. GDP recovering from COVID trough. ~$382 — 34% of peak. Severe decline but improving from 2020.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2021 ~$382

2022.assessment: Oil prices high globally (Ukraine war effect). GDP per capita improving to ~$440 — 40% of peak. Still severe decline from independence peak but best since 2014.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan GDP per capita 2022 ~$440 estimated

2023.assessment: Sudan war (April 2023) disrupted South Sudan oil exports — pipeline runs through Sudan. Oil production disrupted significantly. Sudan war also sent 700,000+ refugees imposing economic burden. GDP expected to contract. Severe decline likely worsening.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Economic Monitor 2023

2024.assessment: Sudan war continued disrupting oil exports. Machar detained. Political instability. Oil revenue losses severe — government unable to fund basic services. GDP contraction. Severe decline, potentially approaching collapse territory.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Economic Brief 2024

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, oil disruptions continuing. GDP trajectory severe decline. Some analysts project potential oil pipeline alternative routes but not implemented. Severe decline.

2005.assessment: Pre-independence southern Sudan used Sudanese pound with limited formal price tracking. Juba consumer prices influenced by GOSS oil revenues and import costs. Estimated moderate-to-elevated inflation driven by oil-fueled demand in Juba and import costs (all goods imported). Estimated 10-20% inflation.

sources.citation: IMF: Sudan CPI estimates covering southern Sudan 2005

2006.assessment: Oil revenues to GOSS expanding rapidly — demand-pull inflation in Juba. Construction boom with imported materials. Estimated inflation moderate to elevated 10-20%.

2007.assessment: Oil revenues at peak for GOSS. Juba import demand high. Global food prices rising 2007-2008. Estimated inflation elevated 15-25%.

2008.assessment: Global food price crisis 2008. Oil revenues peak creating demand. GFC second half. Juba inflation likely very high given import dependence. Estimated 20-30% elevated to high inflation.

sources.citation: Global food price crisis impact estimate 2008

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil price decline reduced GOSS revenues. Reduced demand pressure. Global food prices moderated. Estimated 10-20% elevated inflation.

2010.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Pre-referendum demand. Elections increased government spending. Estimated 10-20% elevated inflation.

2011.assessment: Independence year. New currency (South Sudan Pound) introduced. Pent-up demand from independence celebrations. Supply disruptions. World Bank records ~46% inflation in 2011. Very high inflation.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012. Revenue collapse. Import costs soared as government unable to fund subsidies. World Bank records ~45% inflation. Very high inflation.

2013.assessment: Oil resumed late 2012 — revenues and imports improved. Inflation reduced somewhat first three quarters. December 2013 civil war began escalating inflation. Estimated 25-40% for year. High inflation.

2014.assessment: Civil war disrupted food production and supply chains. Currency depreciation. Import costs rising. Oil production disrupted in conflict zones. Very high inflation — estimated 40-60%.

2015.assessment: Civil war plus global oil price crash. Unity oil fields destroyed — no revenue. SSP began devaluing rapidly. Estimated 50-100% inflation. Very high to hyperinflationary.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. South Sudan Pound lost 90%+ of value — exchange rate collapsed from 2.95 to 80+ per USD. World Bank records ~835% inflation — hyperinflation. Food prices became unaffordable for most citizens. Famine conditions in Unity State. Catastrophic.

2017.assessment: Hyperinflation continued though declining from 2016 peak. Famine declared Unity State. Currency continued depreciating. World Bank records ~187% inflation. Hyperinflation still.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS signed September 2018. Some monetary stabilization attempts. Inflation declining from hyperinflationary peak but still very high. Estimated 60-100%. Very high to hyperinflationary.

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS period. Some economic stabilization. Oil revenues recovering. Inflation declining but still high. Estimated 30-60%. Very high.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 pandemic. Oil price crash. RTGoNU formed February 2020. Economic pressure renewed currency depreciation. Inflation very high. Estimated 30-50%.

2021.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Some monetary stabilization. Inflation reducing but still high. Estimated 25-40%. High.

2022.assessment: High oil prices (Ukraine war effect). Government revenue improved. Some monetary stability. Global food inflation (Ukraine war). Net effect estimated 15-30% elevated-to-high inflation.

2023.assessment: Sudan war April 2023 disrupted oil exports. Revenue losses + 700,000 refugees created inflation pressure. Currency depreciation renewed. Inflation rising. Estimated 30-50% high to very high.

2024.assessment: Sudan war continues disrupting oil exports. Machar detained — political instability renewed currency pressure. Fiscal crisis with revenue losses. Inflation likely very high. Estimated 40-80%.

2025.assessment: March 2025: ongoing Sudan war oil disruption, political instability. Inflation trajectory very high. Estimated very high continuing.

2005.assessment: GOSS oil revenues flowing through Sudan pipeline. South Sudan (as GOSS) entirely dependent on oil exports and imported goods. Trade openness dominated by oil — high ratio if oil counted. Non-oil trade very restricted. Highly open via oil dependency.

2006.assessment: Oil exports through Sudan pipeline growing. Juba imports expanding with oil revenues. Open economy dominated by oil and imports.

2007.assessment: Oil exports at GOSS peak through Sudan. Imports high. Highly open via oil.

2008.assessment: Oil price spike 2008 — oil export value surged. Imports expanded. Highly open.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil price decline. Reduced export value. Imports contracting. Moderate openness.

2010.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Imports growing. Moderate to open economy.

2011.assessment: Independence. Oil exports at ~350,000 bpd. Exports dominated by oil going through Sudan pipeline. Almost all consumer goods imported. Trade-to-GDP ratio very high (estimates suggest >80-100%). Highly open via oil-export dependency.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 — exports ceased for most of year. Trade-to-GDP ratio collapsed. Imports continued but at reduced level due to revenue loss. Restricted trade openness.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Trade 2012 — oil shutdown

2013.assessment: Oil resumed late 2012 and through 2013 — trade openness recovering. Civil war December disrupted economic activity but oil exports mostly continued. Open via oil recovery.

2014.assessment: Civil war disrupted oil production in Unity State. Global oil price fall. Trade openness declining. Moderate openness as war and price decline reduced oil exports.

2015.assessment: Unity oil fields destroyed. Global oil price crashed. Oil export revenue collapsed. Trade openness severely reduced. Restricted.

2016.assessment: Economic collapse. Oil revenues minimal. Hyperinflation. ARCSS collapsed July. Currency collapse meant import purchasing power nearly zero. Very restricted to autarkic conditions in some areas.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Trade 2016 — economic collapse

2017.assessment: Famine. Humanitarian imports replacing commercial imports. Some oil exports resuming. Very restricted trade except humanitarian flows.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Oil production recovering. Trade openness recovering. Restricted to moderate.

2019.assessment: Oil production improving. Imports recovering. Trade openness moderate.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 reduced trade globally. Oil price crash. RTGoNU formed but economic headwinds. Trade openness declining from improving trajectory. Restricted.

2021.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Trade recovering from COVID trough. Moderate openness.

2022.assessment: High oil prices (Ukraine war effect). Oil export revenues high. Imports improving. Moderate to open.

2023.assessment: Sudan war from April 2023 disrupted the oil pipeline. South Sudan oil exports severely impacted. Trade openness declining sharply from 2022. Restricted.

2024.assessment: Sudan war continues disrupting pipeline. Oil exports minimal. Imports constrained by revenue loss. Trade openness severely restricted.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing. Oil pipeline still disrupted. Trade severely restricted to near closed.

2005.assessment: No formal labor market data for southern Sudan. Overwhelming majority (~85%) in subsistence farming and herding. Very limited formal sector — GOSS civil service and small Juba private sector. "Unemployment" by formal definition low but underemployment near universal. Crisis level structural underemployment.

sources.citation: ILO: Sudan Labor Market (covering southern Sudan) 2005

2006.assessment: GOSS oil revenues beginning to create some formal employment in Juba — construction, civil service. But rural majority subsistence. No formal labor tracking. Crisis level underemployment nationally.

sources.citation: World Bank: Southern Sudan Labor Estimate 2006

2007.assessment: Oil-boom Juba creating some formal jobs. Still majority subsistence rural population. Very limited formal labor market. Crisis level underemployment nationally.

2008.assessment: Oil boom Juba employment. Crisis underemployment national level continues. Global food crisis added food insecurity pressure on agricultural workforce. Crisis level underemployment.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil revenues reduced. Less formal employment creation in Juba. Rural subsistence dominant. Crisis underemployment.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum period. Juba expanding. Some census data on occupations. Rural majority subsistence. Crisis underemployment nationally.

2011.assessment: Independence. New state, no formal labor statistics. Formal sector tiny — government civil service and oil sector. 80%+ rural subsistence. Youth unemployment in Juba very high. Crisis level underemployment nationally by any measure.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Labor Market 2011

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012. Oil workers laid off. Civil service salaries delayed. Construction projects halted. Juba formal unemployment spiked. Rural subsistence continued. Crisis level underemployment.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Economic Brief 2012

2013.assessment: Oil resumed — formal employment recovering in first three quarters. December 2013 civil war created massive displacement and unemployment. Crisis underemployment throughout.

2014.assessment: Civil war created mass displacement of 1+ million people by end of 2014. Agricultural disruption — farmers couldn't access fields. Massive unemployment from conflict displacement. Crisis level in all categories.

2015.assessment: Civil war continuing. 2+ million displaced. Oil fields partially offline. Unity State agricultural production zero. Mass unemployment from conflict. Crisis level.

2016.assessment: Economic collapse. Hyperinflation. Currency collapse. Private sector destroyed in Juba fighting. 3+ million displaced (internal and external refugees). Mass unemployment. Crisis level.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Economic Collapse 2016

2017.assessment: Famine in Unity State. 4+ million displaced. War across all regions. Employment essentially nonexistent outside Juba's minimal formal sector. Crisis level.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Some stabilization. Displaced people starting to return in some areas. Still crisis level employment given war destruction. Crisis level.

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS. Some economic activity recovering. Private sector slowly rebuilding in Juba. Still 4+ million displaced. Very high underemployment nationally. Crisis level.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 + unity government + flooding. COVID disrupted Juba economic activity. Flooding displaced 800,000+ from economic activity. Oil crash reduced government employment. Crisis level.

2021.assessment: Some economic recovery from COVID trough. Oil employment recovering. Flooding continued (third consecutive year). Still crisis level underemployment nationally given structural conditions.

2022.assessment: High oil prices improving government revenues. Some employment recovery. Flooding again. Structural underemployment crisis level continues in rural areas and conflict-affected zones.

2023.assessment: Sudan war disrupted oil exports. 700,000+ refugees added to labor market pressure. Oil employment reduced. Government fiscal crisis reduced civil service employment capacity. Crisis level.

2024.assessment: Sudan war continues disrupting oil exports. Fiscal crisis severe — civil service salaries delayed/unpaid. Machar detained — political instability. Crisis level underemployment.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, oil disruption, political instability. Crisis level underemployment continuing.

2005.assessment: GOSS was a semi-autonomous entity — not an independent state. Limited direct diplomatic relations. International donors and NGOs had significant presence supporting CPA implementation. US, EU, UN heavily invested in peace process. No UN seat but significant international engagement. Significant isolation by formal metrics but active informal international engagement.

sources.citation: ICG: Southern Sudan CPA International Engagement 2005

2006.assessment: GOSS attracting significant international presence in Juba. UN, World Bank, bilateral donors with permanent offices. Pre-independence diplomatic status — significant international engagement despite formal limitations.

sources.citation: World Bank: Southern Sudan International Engagement 2006

2007.assessment: GOSS building international profile. Senior diplomats meeting GOSS leadership in Juba. Pre-independence international engagement growing. Significant isolation by formal metrics but partially integrated informally.

2008.assessment: Pre-referendum period. Significant international investment in peace process and southern Sudan development. US particularly engaged with GOSS. Partial isolation by formal status but strong active engagement.

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum preparations. Growing international engagement with GOSS leadership. Juba increasingly international hub. Strong informal integration.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum. Major international presence in Juba. Elections monitored by international observers. GOSS internationally recognized de facto. Active international engagement despite formal non-state status.

2011.assessment: July 2011 independence. Immediate UN admission as 193rd member state. Recognized by 109 countries within first week. Admitted to AU, IGAD. Massive international goodwill. Embassies opened in Juba. Full diplomatic integration at independence.

sources.citation: UN: South Sudan admitted 193rd UN member state July 14 2011

2012.assessment: Full UN/AU/IMF/World Bank member. Oil dispute with Sudan strained bilateral relations but not broader integration. International community mediated oil dispute. Normal international relations.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan full membership 2012

2013.assessment: Normal international relations through November. December 2013 civil war outbreak immediately drew international condemnation. IGAD emergency summits. US/UK evacuated nationals. International community shocked. Rapid deterioration of external standing. From normal to partial isolation.

sources.citation: IGAD: South Sudan Emergency Summit December 2013

2014.assessment: Civil war. International community deeply engaged in peace process (IGAD) but increasingly critical. US/EU sanctions on individuals. Some embassies reduced staff. UNMISS mandate strengthened. Partial isolation as civil war continued. Active but strained international relations.

2015.assessment: ARCSS negotiations dominated international engagement. International community (US, EU, IGAD) deeply involved. Some countries closed embassies. UNMISS present. Obama administration threatened comprehensive sanctions. Partial isolation with some closures.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. International community severely criticized government. US evacuated nationals from Juba. Arms embargo discussion. UN special mission elevated. Significant isolation. Only China and regional neighbors remained close.

2017.assessment: UN arms embargo blocked but US/EU sectoral sanctions. Famine internationally declared. International humanitarian response massive but government-international relations strained. UNMISS. Significant isolation.

2018.assessment: UN arms embargo imposed July 2018 (first passed). R-ARCSS signed September 2018 improved international standing somewhat. International community engaged in peace monitoring. Significant isolation improving with R-ARCSS.

sources.citation: UN: South Sudan Arms Embargo 2428; R-ARCSS 2018

2019.assessment: R-ARCSS pre-transition period. International community engaged supportively. Arms embargo and targeted sanctions maintained. International donors re-engaging cautiously. Partial isolation with some closures — improving from 2016-2017 nadir.

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed February 2020 — internationally celebrated. International community re-engaged significantly. IMF, World Bank resumed engagement. Some embassies reopened or expanded. Still arms embargo and targeted sanctions. Partial isolation with improving engagement.

2021.assessment: Elections postponed. International community critical of delays. IMF program discussions. Aid flowing. Arms embargo renewed. Partial isolation with active engagement.

2022.assessment: Elections postponed again. International community frustrated. IMF engagement. High oil prices improved bilateral relations with some donors. Partial isolation.

2023.assessment: Sudan war complicated South Sudan's international position — became refugee host, also oil disrupted. International community engaged on humanitarian. Elections postponed to 2024. Partial isolation with significant humanitarian engagement.

2024.assessment: Machar detained April 2024 — international community concerned about renewed conflict. Calls from US, EU for his release. Elections postponed to December 2026. Significant isolation risk re-emerging.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Machar detained. International criticism. Sudan war ongoing disrupts oil. Elections 2026 uncertain. Significant isolation.

2005.assessment: CNPC (China), Petronas (Malaysia), ONGC (India) invested in southern Sudan oil sector pre-independence. Oil sector FDI significant. CPA created investment security. FDI concentrated in oil sector. High FDI in oil; nearly no non-oil FDI. High overall due to oil sector concentration.

sources.citation: UNCTAD: Africa Investment Report — Sudan/Southern Sudan 2005

2006.assessment: Oil sector investment growing. CNPC expanding. Construction FDI from Chinese contractors. High FDI as percentage of small southern Sudan GDP.

2007.assessment: Oil sector FDI peak period. GNPOC (Greater Nile Petroleum Operating Company) investments. Chinese infrastructure investment in Juba. High FDI relative to small GDP.

2008.assessment: Oil sector investment continuing. GFC did not immediately impact oil FDI. High FDI.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC oil price decline reduced new investment. Existing oil companies maintaining but not expanding. Moderate to high FDI.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum. Oil prices recovering. Some new investment in anticipation of independence. Moderate to high FDI.

2011.assessment: Independence created FDI surge. Oil companies expanded. New businesses establishing in Juba. International interest. Multiple sectors attracted some investment. High FDI relative to small GDP.

sources.citation: UNCTAD: South Sudan FDI 2011 — independence surge

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 devastated FDI. Oil companies halted investments. Construction projects stopped. FDI collapsed to very low or negative levels. Very low to negative FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan FDI 2012 — oil shutdown

2013.assessment: Oil resumed — FDI recovering in first three quarters. December 2013 civil war began. FDI halted again immediately on civil war news. Low FDI for year.

2014.assessment: Civil war. Most foreign businesses evacuated Juba or halted operations. Oil companies reduced investments. FDI near zero or negative (capital flight). Negative net outflows likely.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan FDI 2014 — civil war

2015.assessment: Civil war plus oil crash. Unity oil fields destroyed. Oil company investment stopped entirely. Capital flight. Negative net outflows.

2016.assessment: Economic collapse, hyperinflation, ARCSS collapse. No FDI possible. Capital flight. Negative net outflows.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan FDI 2016 — collapse

2017.assessment: Famine, war. No FDI. Capital flight continuing. Negative net outflows.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Some oil sector investment recovery. Very low FDI recovering. Minimal.

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS. Oil sector FDI modest recovery. Non-oil FDI minimal. Minimal.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 + oil price crash. RTGoNU formed. FDI declining again. Minimal to negative.

2021.assessment: Oil prices recovering. Some FDI in oil sector. Minimal recovery. Very low.

2022.assessment: High oil prices. Some oil sector FDI recovery. Very low to minimal FDI — still far below pre-war levels.

2023.assessment: Sudan war disrupted pipeline — oil FDI reduced. Political uncertainty. FDI declining. Minimal to negative.

2024.assessment: Sudan war, Machar detained, fiscal crisis. FDI near zero or negative. Capital flight from political instability. Negative net outflows likely.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, political instability. FDI likely negative or near zero.

2005.assessment: UNMIS deployed in southern Sudan from 2005 following CPA. Up to 10,000 peacekeepers with mandate to monitor CPA implementation. International forces invited. UNMIS was a significant invited UN presence. Significant invited forces.

2006.assessment: UNMIS fully deployed. ~10,000 peacekeepers in southern Sudan. GOSS invited. UN monitoring CPA implementation. Significant invited forces.

2007.assessment: UNMIS continuing. ~10,000 forces. GOSS cooperation. CPA monitoring. Significant invited forces.

2008.assessment: UNMIS continuing. Tensions with SAF in Abyei. UNMIS roles included Abyei monitoring. ~10,000 forces. Significant invited forces.

2009.assessment: UNMIS continuing. US AFRICOM began limited engagement in LRA tracking. Pre-referendum period. Significant invited forces.

2010.assessment: UNMIS continuing through referendum preparations. Elections monitored. Limited US AFRICOM LRA counter-operations. Significant invited forces.

2011.assessment: Independence July 2011. UNMIS replaced by UNMISS (UNSC Resolution 1996). UNMISS initially had ~7,000 troops with civilian protection mandate. Uganda deployed troops in support (against LRA). US AFRICOM LRA operations. Significant invited foreign military presence.

2012.assessment: UNMISS continuing. Oil dispute with Sudan raised tensions — SAF forces in contested areas. Uganda troops deployed for LRA counter-operations. Contested mixed presence in some border areas (Abyei, contested border with SAF presence). Contested mixed presence.

2013.assessment: Pre-civil war: UNMISS invited presence. December 2013 civil war: Uganda rapidly deployed troops in support of Kiir government (January 2014 offensive). Uganda's involvement initially invited but significantly changed dynamics. UNMISS unable to stop the conflict. Contested mixed presence — UNMISS neutral, Uganda government-side.

sources.citation: UNMISS; Uganda UPDF deployment South Sudan Dec 2013

2014.assessment: Uganda UPDF ~6,000 troops in South Sudan fighting alongside SPLA against SPLA-IO. UNMISS expanded to 12,500 troops for civilian protection. UNMISS PoC sites hosting 100,000+. Sudan providing material support to SPLA-IO from north. Active foreign combat operations (Uganda) + UNMISS peacekeeping. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Uganda UPDF South Sudan 2014

2015.assessment: Uganda UPDF still present in South Sudan though beginning withdrawal. UNMISS 12,500+ troops with expanded civilian protection mandate. ARCSS signed August 2015 included Uganda withdrawal provisions. Active foreign combat operations (Uganda reducing).

2016.assessment: Uganda withdrew most forces following ARCSS implementation (before collapse). July 2016 ARCSS collapse — Uganda did not redeploy. UNMISS attacked by government forces during July Juba fighting — contested. UNMISS PoC sites under attack. Contested mixed presence as government attacked UN.

sources.citation: UNMISS: South Sudan July 2016 — Terrain Hotel

2017.assessment: UNMISS Regional Protection Force (RPF) authorized August 2016 deployed 2017 — 4,000 additional troops for Juba airport security. Government initially refused RPF entry but allowed eventually. RPF deployed under contested circumstances. Contested mixed presence.

sources.citation: UN: UNMISS Regional Protection Force deployment 2017

2018.assessment: UNMISS with RPF continuing. R-ARCSS September 2018 improved relations with UNMISS. RPF presence normalized. Government-UNMISS relations improved post-R-ARCSS. Significant invited forces.

2019.assessment: UNMISS continuing. RPF in Juba. Pre-transition period. Some UNMISS PoC site closures as peace improved. Significant invited forces.

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed February 2020. UNMISS mandate continued but civilian protection emphasis reduced as peace progressed. PoC site closures. RPF maintained. Significant invited forces.

2021.assessment: UNMISS ~12,000 troops continuing. RPF in Juba. Some PoC sites closing as internally displaced return home. Rwanda contributed troops to RPF. Significant invited forces.

2022.assessment: UNMISS continuing. RPF. PoC sites transitioning. Elections delayed. International military presence normalized as significant invited forces.

2023.assessment: UNMISS continuing. Sudan war from April 2023 added pressure — South Sudan border with Sudan stressed. 700,000+ refugees. UNMISS mandate remained strong. Significant invited forces.

2024.assessment: UNMISS continuing with elevated concern after Machar detention April 2024. Sudan war ongoing affecting South Sudan. Some concern about renewed conflict — UNMISS mandate reinforced. Significant invited forces.

2025.assessment: March 2025: UNMISS continuing. Political tensions from Machar detention. Sudan war ongoing. Significant invited foreign military presence with UNMISS.

2005.assessment: Pre-independence. CPA triggered return of southern Sudanese refugees from Uganda, Kenya, Ethiopia. Hundreds of thousands returning to southern Sudan. South Sudan was a net destination for returnees. Net returns dominate over any outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR: Southern Sudan 2005 — CPA return migration

2006.assessment: Return migration continuing. UNHCR assisted returns from Uganda and Kenya. LRA violence in Equatoria caused some internal displacement. Net returns dominate.

2007.assessment: Return migration from diaspora continuing. LRA attacks in western Equatoria causing localized displacement. Net returns from CPA period still dominant.

2008.assessment: LRA escalated attacks in Equatoria 2008 causing significant displacement. Return migration from CPA period slowing. Internal displacement from LRA and inter-communal violence. Moderate new outflows from LRA disruption.

2009.assessment: LRA attacks continuing. Inter-communal violence (Lou Nuer vs Murle) causing internal displacement. Some return migration from diaspora. Moderate outflows from violence.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum. Some return migrants coming back in anticipation of independence. Inter-communal violence continuing. Net minimal displacement — returns and outflows roughly balanced with slight net inflow.

2011.assessment: Independence July 2011 triggered major return migration surge. Hundreds of thousands returned from Uganda, Kenya, Ethiopia, Sudan. Net returns dominate — net returns and minimal displacement overall.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown economic crisis caused some reverse migration. Ongoing inter-communal violence in Jonglei (Murle, Lou Nuer). 100,000+ IDPs. Significant outflows from violence — 1-3% of population range.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2012 — Jonglei violence IDP

2013.assessment: December 2013 civil war outbreak. 400,000+ fled to neighboring countries. 700,000+ internally displaced by year-end. Total approximately 1.1 million displaced — about 9% of 12M population. Mass displacement crossing into catastrophic threshold.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan civil war displacement 2013

2014.assessment: Civil war in full swing. 1.6 million+ refugees in Uganda, Ethiopia, Kenya, Sudan, CAR, DRC. 1.5 million+ internally displaced. Total displaced exceeded 3 million — over 25% of 12 million population. Catastrophic displacement.

2015.assessment: 2 million refugees in neighboring countries. 1.8 million+ IDPs. Total displaced approximately 3.8 million — over 30% of population. Catastrophic displacement continuing.

2016.assessment: July 2016 ARCSS collapse and Juba fighting caused new massive outflow. UNHCR called it the fastest growing refugee crisis. 2.5 million+ refugees. 2 million+ IDPs. Total approaching 4.5 million — ~37% of population. Catastrophic.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2016 — world's fastest growing refugee crisis

2017.assessment: Peak displacement crisis. 3.7 million refugees abroad — world's third largest refugee situation. 2 million+ IDPs. Famine declared. Total displaced approximately 6 million out of 12 million population — 50%. Catastrophic.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2017 — 3.7 million refugees, world's third largest

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018 began reducing outflows. Refugee numbers stabilized around 2.5 million. IDPs remained 2 million+. Total approximately 4.5 million — 37% of population. Catastrophic still.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2018 — stabilizing at 2.5 million

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS return migration beginning. Refugee numbers declining — approximately 2.2 million. Some organized voluntary returns. IDPs declining. Total displacement still approximately 4 million — ~33% of population. Catastrophic.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2019 — 2.2 million refugees, returns beginning

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed February 2020. COVID-19 halted organized returns. Refugee numbers remained approximately 2.2 million. Total displacement approximately 3.8 million — ~30% of population. Catastrophic.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2020 — COVID halted returns

2021.assessment: Post-COVID some return migration resumed. Refugee numbers approximately 2.2 million. Localized conflicts continued producing new IDPs. Net total displacement still approximately 3.5-4 million — ~30% of population. Catastrophic.

2022.assessment: Displacement situation stabilized but remained large. 2.2 million refugees. 1.9 million IDPs. Total approximately 4.1 million — ~33% of population. Elections postponed. Catastrophic.

2023.assessment: Sudan war April 2023 sent 700,000+ Sudanese refugees INTO South Sudan — inverting the historical pattern. South Sudan both a source (2.3 million abroad) and a major recipient. Total displacement including hosted refugees exceeds 5 million — catastrophic bidirectional crisis.

sources.citation: UNHCR: South Sudan 2023 — host to 700,000+ Sudanese while 2.3 million abroad

2024.assessment: Sudan war ongoing — Sudanese refugee influx continuing to South Sudan (800,000+). South Sudanese abroad approximately 2.3 million. Machar detention created renewed conflict risk. Total displacement exceeds 5 million. Catastrophic bidirectional.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, Machar detained, political instability. Sudanese refugee influx continuing. South Sudanese diaspora large. Total displacement catastrophic.

2005.assessment: Southern Sudan as part of Sudan subject to US comprehensive sanctions on Sudan (since 1997). UN had targeted sanctions on Sudan (Resolution 1556 - 2004, Darfur-related). However, US sanctions had exemptions for southern Sudan given CPA support. Practically, southern Sudan faced limited direct sanctions impact. Broad economic/humanitarian exceptions applied.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: Sudan Sanctions Program (covering southern Sudan)

2006.assessment: Sudan sanctions still technically applied to southern Sudan but US OFAC licenses allowed GOSS activity. Oil companies operating in GOSS oil fields granted specific licenses. Broad humanitarian exceptions. Limited direct sanctions impact on GOSS.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: Sudan Sanctions Exemptions 2006

2007.assessment: Continued broad exceptions for GOSS from Sudan sanctions regime. Oil companies in GOSS licensed. International community supportive of GOSS. Limited sanctions impact.

2008.assessment: Pre-independence GOSS largely shielded from Sudan sanctions. US policy strongly supportive of southern Sudanese independence path. Broad exceptions.

2009.assessment: Pre-independence preparations. GOSS effectively exempted from Sudan sanctions impact. Broad exceptions.

2010.assessment: US announced it would ease Sudan sanctions if 2011 referendum went smoothly. GOSS effectively unsanctioned. Broad exceptions or no effective sanctions.

2011.assessment: Independence July 2011. South Sudan no longer covered by Sudan sanctions. US, EU, and international community extended full diplomatic recognition without sanctions. South Sudan immediately admitted to UN, AU, IGAD. No sanctions against new state. No sanctions.

sources.citation: US Treasury: South Sudan — no sanctions program 2011

2012.assessment: No sanctions on South Sudan. Oil shutdown dispute with Sudan — international community encouraged resolution. No punitive measures against GOSS. No sanctions.

sources.citation: US Treasury: South Sudan — no sanctions 2012

2013.assessment: No sanctions before civil war. December 2013 civil war outbreak — international community issued condemnations but no immediate sanctions. IGAD mediation launched. No formal sanctions imposed in 2013.

sources.citation: US Treasury: South Sudan — no sanctions 2013

2014.assessment: May 2014: US imposed targeted sanctions on several South Sudanese warlords/military commanders (Peter Gadet, Marial Chanuong). Not comprehensive country sanctions. UNSC discussed but China/Russia blocked comprehensive measures. Targeted individuals only.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: South Sudan-Related Sanctions 2014

2015.assessment: US expanded targeted sanctions list to additional individuals. Obama administration threatened comprehensive sanctions if peace deal not signed. ARCSS signed August 2015 partly to avoid comprehensive sanctions. Targeted individuals expanded.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: South Sudan Targeted Sanctions 2015

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. US, EU discussed expanded sanctions. Obama executive order allowed broader targeted sanctions on South Sudan in August 2016. EU imposed arms embargo. Additional individuals sanctioned. Sectoral/targeted expanding.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: South Sudan Sanctions 2016

2017.assessment: US expanded targeted sanctions list further (Kiir, Machar, other commanders). EU maintained arms embargo. UNSC vetoed arms embargo at UN level (Russia/China). Sectoral sanctions on arms trade in effect from EU/US. Targeted/sectoral.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC: South Sudan Sanctions 2017

2018.assessment: July 2018: UN Security Council Resolution 2428 imposed UN arms embargo on South Sudan — first time this passed with China/Russia support. US, EU, and UN now all with sanctions measures. R-ARCSS signed September 2018 — sanctions pressure contributed. Sectoral sanctions (arms) from UN plus targeted individuals from US/EU.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 2428 July 2018 — Arms Embargo
US Treasury OFAC: South Sudan Sanctions 2018

2019.assessment: UN arms embargo in effect. US targeted sanctions maintained. EU arms embargo. R-ARCSS pre-transition — international community maintained pressure. Sectoral sanctions (arms) + targeted individuals.

sources.citation: UN: South Sudan Arms Embargo 2019 continuation

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed February 2020. UN arms embargo renewed but with exemptions for government forces. US targeted sanctions maintained. Some easing discussed given peace progress but sanctions in force. Sectoral (arms) + targeted.

sources.citation: UN: South Sudan Arms Embargo 2020 renewal

2021.assessment: UN arms embargo renewed annually. US targeted list maintained. Elections postponed. International community maintaining pressure. Sectoral (arms) + targeted.

2022.assessment: UN arms embargo renewed. US targeted sanctions maintained. EU arms embargo. Elections delayed to 2023+. Sectoral + targeted.

2023.assessment: UN arms embargo renewed. Sudan war added pressure — South Sudan-Sudan relations complicated. US/EU targeted sanctions maintained. Sectoral + targeted.

2024.assessment: UN arms embargo renewed. Machar detained April 2024 — calls for stronger measures. US targeted sanctions maintained. International community concerned about renewed conflict risk. Sectoral + targeted.

2025.assessment: March 2025: UN arms embargo and targeted sanctions in force. Political instability since Machar detention. Sectoral + targeted.

2005.assessment: Southern Sudan under GOSS/SPLA had severe civil liberties restrictions. SPLA acted as both military and governing authority. No free press. Assembly restricted by security environment and SPLA. Arbitrary detention common. LRA attacks restricted movement in Equatoria. Freedom House rated Sudan overall CL=7 (worst). No civil liberties.

2006.assessment: GOSS establishing itself. Some improvement from full Khartoum control but SPLA dominance of civil life. No independent media. SPLA arbitrary detention. Religious freedom somewhat better than northern Sudan. FH Sudan CL=7. No civil liberties.

2007.assessment: GOSS building civil society infrastructure. Some NGOs operating in Juba. Press freedom very limited. SPLA retains civil authority. Arbitrary detention common. Some religious freedoms. Very limited but slight improvement from worst. Severe restrictions.

2008.assessment: Press starting — some independent newspapers in Juba but not durable. GOSS security services active. Arbitrary detention. Religious freedom better than north. Census conducted — some assembly around that. Severe restrictions.

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum period. Growing civil society in Juba. Some press freedom. SPLA/GOSS security arbitrary detention. Violence context restricts movement/assembly in conflict areas. Severe restrictions overall.

2010.assessment: 2010 elections — some political freedom exercised. Civil society growing in Juba. Press operates but with harassment. Arbitrary detention by SPLA continuing. Pre-referendum atmosphere. Significant restrictions but some political space.

2011.assessment: Independence brought initial optimism. Freedom House began separate coverage. CL=5. Referendum was celebrated, civil society active. Press freedom in Juba — some newspapers, radio. However SPLA/NSSS (National Security) arbitrary detention. Multiple rebel groups restricted movement in states. Significant restrictions overall.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown crisis. Press closures — government shut newspapers that criticized policy. Civil society harassed. NSSS arbitrary detention increased. Dissidents arrested. Freedom House CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2013.assessment: December 2013 civil war. Massacre of Nuer civilians in Juba. Journalists detained. Civil society targeted. Emergency conditions. Freedom House CL=7. No civil liberties.

2014.assessment: Full civil war. Mass atrocities. No press freedom. Journalists arrested. Civil society unable to operate. Arbitrary killings. Mass rape as weapon of war. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2015.assessment: Civil war continued. Systematic abuses. ARCSS signed but not implemented. Civil society under threat. Media closed. UNMISS reported mass sexual violence. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. New offensives. Sexual violence against aid workers at Terrain Hotel July 2016 — documented by UN. Civil society targeted. Press freedom non-existent. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2017.assessment: War across all regions. Famine. Media workers killed. Civil society unable to operate. Arbitrary detention. Mass atrocities. Arms embargo debate. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS signed September 2018 — some hope. But civil liberties remained non-existent. Media workers continued to be arrested. Civil society harassed. NSSS arbitrary detentions. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2019.assessment: Pre-transition period. Journalists continued to be arrested. Civil society restricted. NSSS arbitrary detention. Sexual violence persisting. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed February 2020. Some slight improvement in political space in Juba. Media workers still harassed. NSSS detention continuing. Flooding displaced 800,000+. FH maintained CL=7. No civil liberties.

2021.assessment: Civil liberties remained non-existent. Journalists arrested, media closed. NSSS arbitrary detention. Inter-communal violence restricted movement. Sexual violence in conflict areas. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2022.assessment: Civil liberties non-existent. Media workers arrested, publications suspended. NSSS arbitrary detention. Inter-communal violence restricts movement in multiple states. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2023.assessment: Civil liberties non-existent. Journalists arrested. Civil society under threat. NSSS arbitrary detention. Sudan war refugees added displacement pressure. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2024.assessment: Machar detained April 2024. Political arrests including opposition figures. Civil society severely restricted. Media under surveillance and harassment. No civil liberties.

2025.assessment: March 2025: civil liberties remain non-existent. Machar remains detained. Political space closed. Media restricted. NSSS arbitrary detention continues. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: South Sudan early 2025

2005.assessment: GOSS under John Garang (killed July 2005) then Salva Kiir. SPLM coalition relatively unified under Garang around CPA signing. Garang's death created leadership transition — Kiir took over. Some tension between SPLM factions. Garang's widow Nyandeng and others created some factional tension. SPLM broadly unified around CPA implementation. Significant competition but managed.

2006.assessment: Kiir consolidated leadership. SPLM unified around CPA implementation and oil revenue management. Some tension with SSDF (former SAF-aligned southern forces) integration. Overall SPLM elite broadly unified. Significant competition managed.

2007.assessment: SPLM internal debates over governance and oil revenue use (massive corruption). Kiir and Machar in government together. Some elite competition over patronage network. Unified around independence goal. Significant competition managed around independence aspiration.

2008.assessment: Pre-referendum preparations. SPLM leadership competitive but managing differences. Kiir as consensus figure. Oil crash reduced patronage resources increasing competition. Corruption-driven factionalism growing. Significant competition managed.

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum period. Kiir-Machar relationship functional. SPLM elite broadly unified around referendum goal. Competition for post-independence positions growing. George Athor rebel — disaffected elite not fully in SPLM orbit. Significant competition managed with some open elements.

2010.assessment: 2010 elections — Kiir won; Machar as VP. Some SPLM candidates ran as independents after losing primaries. George Athor rebellion post-election. SPLM elite broadly unified but election revealed fissures. Significant competition with open factional elements.

2011.assessment: Independence triumph united SPLM elite temporarily. Kiir as President, Machar as VP. Multiple rebel groups outside SPLM orbit still fighting (Athor, Yau Yau). SPLM leadership broadly unified for independence celebrations. Post-independence competition for resources beginning. Significant competition managed.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown crisis strained elite relations — blame game for economic disaster. SPLM factional tensions growing around corruption and resource allocation. Kiir-Machar relationship showing strain. Corruption patronage competing. Open factional conflict within SPLM emerging.

2013.assessment: July 2013: Kiir dismissed entire cabinet including Machar. Machar announced presidential candidacy. SPLM political bureau met and split. December 2013: Kiir accused Machar of coup — civil war. Elite cohesion completely shattered. Active power struggles with violent consequences. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: South Sudan December 2013

2014.assessment: Civil war. Kiir led SPLA, Machar led SPLA-IO. SPLM irrevocably split. IGAD mediation ongoing. Each side fragmented further — SPLA-IO internally divided. Multiple competing authorities. Complete fragmentation.

2015.assessment: ARCSS negotiations. Kiir faction split — some SPLM members supported negotiated peace (SPLM-FDs). SPLA-IO further fragmented with multiple commanders defecting back to government. Complete fragmentation with ARCSS offering nominal reunification path.

2016.assessment: TGoNU briefly formed April 2016 with Machar as First VP — very brief period of nominal reunification. July 2016 Juba fighting ended unity attempt. Machar fled. Elite further fragmented — Thomas Cirillo, Taban Deng (replaced Machar in TGoNU), and others. Complete fragmentation.

2017.assessment: Multiple competing armed factions: government/SPLA, SPLA-IO (Machar's rump), SSNA (Cirillo), smaller groups. Kiir's own government internally contested — Taban Deng faction. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts South Sudan 2017

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS signed September 2018 — Kiir and Machar reconciled nominally. High Commissioner's involvement. Pre-transition period of nominal reunification. Active power struggles continuing beneath surface. Active power struggles.

2019.assessment: Pre-transition period. Kiir-Machar negotiations over power sharing. Multiple iterations of extension. Some competing factions not in deal. Active power struggles around government formation.

2020.assessment: February 2020 RTGoNU formed — Kiir as President, Machar as First VP (4th time). Nominal elite reunification. SSNA (Cirillo) remained outside. Inter-elite competition continuing within RTGoNU over ministries and security forces. Open factional conflict simmering beneath nominal unity.

2021.assessment: RTGoNU continuing. Elections postponed. Peace implementation slow — security arrangements contested. Kiir and Machar publicly aligned but mutual distrust. Competition over military unification commands. Open factional conflict managed under umbrella.

2022.assessment: Continued RTGoNU tensions. Elections postponed to December 2024 then further. Permanent constitution process stalled. Military integration not complete. Inter-party competition. Open factional conflict.

2023.assessment: Sudan war disrupted oil revenues — increased competition for reduced resources. Elections postponed to December 2024. Kiir-Machar tensions growing. Sudan refugees added pressure. Open factional conflict with active power struggle elements.

2024.assessment: Machar detained April 2024 — peace agreement fundamentally threatened. SPLA-IO split between those supporting continued peace and hardliners. Active power struggle between Kiir faction and Machar's detained faction. Most serious elite cohesion crisis since R-ARCSS. Active power struggles potentially leading to complete fragmentation.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Machar remains detained. Elite cohesion fragile. R-ARCSS under severe strain. Active power struggles between Kiir's government and SPLA-IO factions. Elections target December 2026 — competition over position intensifying.

2005.assessment: GOSS established July 2005 under CPA. Semi-autonomous government of southern Sudan with president (Kiir), legislature, and basic ministries. Institutional capacity extremely low — most civil servants untrained, no bureaucratic tradition. Courts essentially nonexistent outside Juba. Legislature met but had minimal policy function. Severely degraded from birth.

2006.assessment: GOSS continued building from near zero. Oil revenues beginning to flow — some capacity building possible. Legislature operational but not law-making substantively. Courts in Juba only. Civil service being staffed but with minimal qualifications. Severely degraded institutions.

sources.citation: World Bank: Southern Sudan Economic Brief 2006

2007.assessment: Capacity building ongoing with international support. GOSS oil revenues ($1.2B+) provided resources but also corruption opportunities. Legislature more active. State governments weak. Customary courts in rural areas outside formal system. Severely degraded but improving.

2008.assessment: GOSS institutions still weak but growing. Oil price crash 2008 reduced revenues. Census conducted. Formal institutions building — Anti-Corruption Commission established nominally. State governments beginning function. Legislature passing laws. Severely degraded but measurable improvement.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Institutional Development 2008

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum preparations. Election commission established. Military integration ongoing but troubled. Courts began functioning in more state capitals. Civil service payroll growing. Legislature more active. Severely degraded but institutions at most basic level beginning to function.

2010.assessment: April 2010 elections — imperfect but held. Legislature elected. Institutional capacity still very low. Corruption massive ($4B embezzled identified by Kiir's own government). Courts functioning nominally. Severely degraded but election was milestone.

2011.assessment: July 2011 independence. New state institutions from scratch — Transitional Constitution of South Sudan. National Legislature, judiciary, executive established formally. UN admission. However, institutional capacity remained very low. Civil service nascent. Courts functioning only in Juba. Military unreformed. Severely degraded despite formal institutional structure.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 devastated government revenues. Austerity crisis. Civil service salaries delayed. Government focused on security not institution building. Legislature functioning but reduced budget for implementation. Courts remained weak. Severely degraded.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Economic Situation 2012

2013.assessment: Pre-civil war (pre-December): institutions functioning at minimal level. Kiir dismissed entire cabinet in July 2013. Political infighting paralyzed government. Legislature barely functioning. December 2013 civil war began — government institutions effectively collapsed in conflict zones. Collapsed or nominal by year end.

2014.assessment: Civil war. State institutions functioned only in Juba and government-held areas. Courts, legislature, civil service effectively nonexistent in conflict zones. IGAD peace negotiations — institutions focused on survival not governance. Multiple areas under SPLA-IO control with no government services. Collapsed or nominal — no functioning institutions in most of country.

2015.assessment: Civil war ongoing. Government institutions functional only in Juba and a few government-held state capitals. Legislature met but focused on war. Courts nonfunctional in most of country. ARCSS signed August 2015 creating new institutional framework on paper not implemented. No functioning institutions in most of country.

2016.assessment: ARCSS partially implemented April-July 2016. Transitional Government of National Unity (TGoNU) briefly formed. July 2016 collapse. Equatoria fell into conflict. Government essentially a Juba-based entity conducting military operations. Legislature rubber-stamping. No functioning institutions across most of country.

2017.assessment: Government declared State of Emergency in Unity State. Legislature operating as rubber stamp. Courts not functioning meaningfully. Civil service not receiving salaries regularly (hyperinflation). International humanitarian organizations providing most services. No functioning institutions in most of country.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts South Sudan 2017

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS signed September 2018. New institutional framework on paper. Transitional period 8-month pre-transition period. Legislature continuing. Some institutions in Juba operating. Outside Juba, minimal functioning. Collapsed or nominal.

2019.assessment: Pre-transition extension granted. Unity government delayed to February 2020. Transitional institutions on paper. Legislature operating. Some bureaucratic functions in Juba. State governments extremely weak. Courts nominal. Collapsed or nominal.

2020.assessment: February 2020 Revitalized Transitional Government of National Unity (RTGoNU) formed. Machar as First VP. Institutional building resumed. Legislature reconstituted with expanded membership per R-ARCSS. Courts still weak but more operational in Juba. Civil service functioning in Juba. Severely degraded but measurable improvement from war-period collapse.

2021.assessment: RTGoNU continuing. Elections postponed to 2022 (later extended further). Reconstituted National Legislature functioning nominally. Peace implementation delayed on many benchmarks (security arrangements, constitution). Courts operational in Juba and some state capitals. Civil service functioning partially. Severely degraded institutions.

2022.assessment: Transitional period extended again. Elections pushed to 2023 (later 2024, then 2026). Legislature operating. Permanent Constitution process stalled. Courts functioning in Juba. IMF engagement. Civil service salaries delayed by fiscal pressure. Severely degraded but more stable than civil war period.

2023.assessment: Sudan war added massive pressure. 700,000+ refugees arrived. Oil revenues disrupted — pipeline through Sudan. Elections postponed to December 2024. Legislature operating but focused on survival politics. Courts in Juba functioning nominally. Institutions under severe strain from refugee crisis and fiscal pressure. Severely degraded.

2024.assessment: Machar arrested/detained April 2024 — peace agreement threatened. Elections postponed to December 2026. Institutional functions in Juba continuing. Legislature operating. Renewed political tensions threatening institutional stability. Constitution process stalled. Severely degraded to collapsed or nominal depending on metric.

2025.assessment: March 2025: transitional institutions continue. RTGoNU functioning in Juba. Political tensions from Machar's detention. Sudan war still disrupting oil revenues and sending refugees. Elections target December 2026 but uncertain. Institutions severely degraded, dependent on international support.

2005.assessment: Post-CPA period. Violence reduced from full civil war levels but significant conflict continued. LRA attacks in Western and Eastern Equatoria. Inter-communal cattle raiding violence particularly acute in Jonglei. Armed groups not integrated into SPLA active. UCDP estimates several thousand conflict deaths. High violence level.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Sudan/Southern Sudan 2005

2006.assessment: GOSS establishing itself. Malakal fighting between SPLA and SAF-backed forces. LRA attacks. Inter-communal violence in Jonglei massive — Nuer-Dinka clashes with hundreds killed. UCDP counts significant battle deaths. Overall estimated 2,000-5,000 deaths from all conflict. High violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Southern Sudan 2006

2007.assessment: Jonglei state inter-communal violence escalated significantly. Lou Nuer-Murle conflicts with thousands killed. LRA continued attacks. Malakal conflict with SSDF forces. UCDP estimates 2,000-6,000 deaths. High violence continuing.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Southern Sudan 2007

2008.assessment: Abyei incidents and rising tension. Jonglei inter-communal violence continued. LRA Christmas massacre December 2008. Multiple cattle raiding conflicts. UCDP conflict deaths estimated 2,000-5,000. High violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Southern Sudan 2008
Human Rights Watch: LRA Christmas Massacre 2008

2009.assessment: One of worst years for inter-communal violence. Jonglei: Lou Nuer attack on Murle in Pibor killed 400+ civilians. Abyei Misseriya-Dinka clashes. LRA attacks. UCDP and ACLED combined: 2,000-8,000 deaths. High violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Southern Sudan 2009

2010.assessment: 2010 elections sparked violence. George Athor rebellion in Jonglei. LRA attacks in Equatoria. Inter-communal violence continued. Multiple armed group clashes. 2,000-5,000 estimated deaths. High violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: Southern Sudan 2010

2011.assessment: Independence year. Multiple armed groups fighting government: Athor rebellion in Jonglei, Yau Yau in Pibor, LRA in Equatoria. Abyei occupied by SAF. 2,000-6,000 deaths estimated. High violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset: South Sudan 2011

2012.assessment: Jonglei: December 2011-January 2012 Lou Nuer attack on Pibor killed 900+ civilians. Border conflict with Sudan (Heglig). Yau Yau rebellion. LRA attacks. Oil shutdown security vacuum. 3,000-7,000 estimated deaths. High violence.

2013.assessment: Moderate violence pre-December. December 15-16 2013: Juba massacre of Nuer civilians — thousands killed in first days. Fighting spread to Jonglei (Bor) and Unity (Bentiu). Civil war ignited. Total 2013 deaths including December: 10,000-15,000+. Severe violence.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: South Sudan December 2013

2014.assessment: Full civil war. SPLA-IO offensives across Greater Upper Nile. Bentiu April 2014 massacre at UN site and mosque. Malakal multiple attacks. UNMISS estimates tens of thousands killed. Some estimates 50,000+ deaths in 2014. Catastrophic violence.

sources.citation: UNMISS: Human Rights Reports South Sudan 2014

2015.assessment: Civil war at severe levels. Unity State oil field attacks. PoC site attacks. Mass sexual violence. ARCSS signed August 2015. 15,000-30,000 deaths estimated. Severe violence.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. Equatoria new war theater. Malakal PoC attack February. Government forces sexual violence/extrajudicial killings documented by UNMISS. 20,000-50,000 deaths. Catastrophic violence.

2017.assessment: War in all regions. Government Equatoria offensive brutal. Famine declared Unity State February 2017. 15,000-30,000 direct conflict deaths with famine mortality higher. Severe violence.

2018.assessment: Fighting reducing toward R-ARCSS signing September 2018. Some ceasefires holding. Equatoria still active. Inter-communal in Jonglei/Warrap. 5,000-15,000 deaths year overall. High but declining violence.

2019.assessment: Post-R-ARCSS, significant reduction in large-scale conflict. Inter-communal violence intensified — Jonglei, Pibor, Warrap, Lakes. SSNA (Cirillo) fighting in Equatoria. 3,000-6,000 deaths. High but improved from civil war peak.

2020.assessment: Unity government formed February 2020. Organized conflict significantly reduced. Inter-communal in Jonglei, Lakes, Warrap continues at high levels. Flooding 800,000+ displaced. 3,000-5,000 deaths. High violence persisting.

2021.assessment: Jonglei/Pibor massive inter-communal attacks — 350+ killed in single event series. Unity State conflict. Warrap cattle raiding. SSNA Equatoria. 2,000-5,000 deaths. High violence.

2022.assessment: Inter-communal violence Jonglei/Warrap/Unity. SSNA fighting Equatoria. Flooding third year. 2,000-4,000 deaths. High but slightly improving from peak civil war.

2023.assessment: Sudan war sent 700,000+ refugees into South Sudan. Inter-communal violence continued. Political tensions over postponed elections. 2,000-4,000 conflict deaths. High violence.

2024.assessment: Machar detained April 2024 — elevated risk of renewed civil war. Upper Nile factional fighting. Inter-communal in Jonglei/Pibor. Sudan war refugees continuing. 2,000-5,000 deaths with elevated risk of escalation. High violence.

2025.assessment: March 2025: inter-communal and factional conflict continue. Political tensions following Machar 2024 detention. High violence baseline. 2,000+ deaths trajectory for 2025. High violence level.

2005.assessment: South Sudan (as southern Sudan) was governed by the SPLM/A under the Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) signed January 2005. The GOSS (Government of Southern Sudan) exercised nominal authority but large portions of the south remained contested or under parallel armed group control. Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) operated in the southeast. Inter-communal violence across Jonglei and other states. Multiple armed groups not fully integrated into SPLA. Government control significant but contested in many areas.

sources.citation: ICG: Southern Sudan: Keeping Peace With the NCP (2005)

2006.assessment: GOSS established in July 2005 and consolidating authority through 2006. SPLA unification efforts ongoing with mixed armed groups. Malakal clashes with SPLA-allied and northern-backed groups. Significant portions of Eastern Equatoria, Jonglei, and Unity State had contested government presence. LRA continued raids. Large portions contested.

2007.assessment: GOSS continued consolidation. Oil revenues from Unity State beginning to fund government. Malakal conflict between SPLA and SAF-backed southern forces. Inter-communal cattle raiding violence in Jonglei acute. Significant areas of northern Unity State and Upper Nile under contested control. Large portions outside full GOSS control.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: The Challenges Ahead (2008)

2008.assessment: GOSS authority improving but still contested. Census completed under difficulty. Malakal back under SPLA control but disputed. SAF/northern-backed forces in parts of Upper Nile and Abyei unresolved. LRA and other armed groups active in southern Equatoria and Jonglei. Significant areas outside GOSS effective control. Large portions contested.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: The Challenges Ahead 2008

2009.assessment: February 2009 Abyei clashes. Jonglei mass atrocities between Lou Nuer and Murle communities. LRA attacks in Western Equatoria killed hundreds. GOSS authority still limited in remote areas. Multiple armed groups active. Significant territorial control gaps. Large portions contested or beyond GOSS effective reach.

2010.assessment: April 2010 elections — Kiir won presidency of GOSS. Referendum preparations underway. George Athor rebellion after losing election in Jonglei — dissident SPLA officers taking up arms. LRA continuing attacks in Equatoria. Multiple armed groups active. GOSS authority nominal in remote areas. Significant areas outside control — large portions contested.

sources.citation: ICG: Southern Sudan: Compendium of Tensions (2010)

2011.assessment: January 2011 independence referendum (98.8% for independence). July 2011 South Sudan became world's newest state. Government of South Sudan (GOSS → Republic of South Sudan) established. Multiple rebel groups active at independence: George Athor in Jonglei, David Yau Yau in Pibor, others. Abyei occupied by SAF in May — not resolved. Multiple competing authorities in many areas. Large portions contested.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: Independence and Beyond (2011)

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012 (South Sudan shut pipeline after fee dispute with Sudan) — fiscal crisis. Jonglei state deteriorated with SPLA disarmament campaigns provoking Yau Yau rebellion. Border fighting with Sudan (Heglig/Panthou crisis). Multiple armed groups active. Government authority contested in Jonglei, Unity State, parts of Equatoria. Large portions contested or outside government control.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: Jonglei's Tribal Conflicts (2012)

2013.assessment: December 2013 political crisis began: Kiir accused Machar of coup attempt; SPLA split along Dinka/Nuer lines. Fighting spread rapidly across Juba, then Unity State, Jonglei, and Upper Nile. By end of 2013, government had lost control of Bentiu (Unity State) and Bor (Jonglei). Civil war underway. Majority of the country effectively outside central government control. Failed state conditions emerging.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: Civil War Risks (2013-2014)
Human Rights Watch: South Sudan December 2013

2014.assessment: Full civil war. SPLA-IO (Machar) controlled much of Greater Upper Nile — Bentiu, Malakal changed hands multiple times. Akobo, Nassir under SPLA-IO. Government held Juba, most of Equatoria, and Western Bahr el Ghazal. Humanitarian catastrophe — UNMISS protecting 100,000+ in PoC sites. Multiple competing authorities across most of the country. Majority outside effective government control.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: Keeping Faith with the IGAD Peace Process (2014)

2015.assessment: Civil war continued. Massive human rights abuses including siege of Malakal, Unity State oil field destruction by SPLA-IO. August 2015 ARCSS peace agreement signed but implementation stalled. SPLA-IO controlled most of Unity State and Upper Nile. Government forces in Juba, Lakes, Equatoria, Warrap, Northern Bahr el Ghazal. Majority of country outside central control with multiple armed authorities.

2016.assessment: April 2016 Machar returned to Juba as First VP under ARCSS. July 2016 fighting at First VP's residence and State House — ARCSS collapsed. Machar fled Juba. New offensive against SPLA-IO. Equatoria fell into conflict — previously stable regions now affected. War spread to all 10 states. Massive displacement. Multiple armed factions. Majority outside control.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: The Politics of Rules (2016)
Human Rights Watch: South Sudan July 2016

2017.assessment: Government attempted reconsolidation. February 2017 Equatoria offensives. Continued fighting in Greater Upper Nile, Greater Equatoria, Greater Bahr el Ghazal now affected. SPLA-IO fragmented but continued. UN Panel of Experts documented government attacks on civilians. Multiple armed groups. Majority of country outside effective central control, multiple competing authorities.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on South Sudan 2017

2018.assessment: September 2018 Revitalized ARCSS (R-ARCSS) signed. Pre-transition period. Fighting reduced but not ended. Multiple armed groups still active. Government held Juba and state capitals nominally. R-ARCSS created new governance arrangements not yet implemented. Significant areas outside effective control. Large portions still contested with some majority-outside pockets.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: R-ARCSS and the Peace Process (2018)

2019.assessment: R-ARCSS transitional period. Formation of unity government delayed. Multiple armed groups continued operations. South Sudan National Democratic Alliance and National Salvation Front not part of R-ARCSS. Fighting reduced from peak civil war levels but persistent. Government nominally controls state capitals and major towns. Rural areas largely beyond government reach. Large portions contested.

2020.assessment: February 2020 unity government formed — Machar returned as First VP. Nominal peace. However, localized fighting continued in several states (Jonglei, Unity, Western Equatoria). Inter-communal violence replaced organized civil war in intensity. Government authority expanded somewhat. Flooding (worst in decades) complicated government presence. Significant areas outside control but improvement from civil war peak. Large portions contested.

sources.citation: ICG: South Sudan: Tending the Complex Peace Process (2020)

2021.assessment: Unity government continued. Inter-communal violence intensified in Jonglei and Greater Pibor Admin Area between Murle and Nuer communities — thousands killed. Unity State conflict continued. Government authority improved in major towns but rural areas remain largely ungoverned. Significant areas outside control. Large portions contested.

2022.assessment: Persistent localized conflict across multiple states. Jonglei inter-communal violence continued. Unity State fighting. Government extended state authority in some areas. Persistent flooding for third consecutive year displaced hundreds of thousands. SSNA (South Sudan National Alliance) attacks in Western Equatoria. Government controls Juba and state capitals, nominal control of counties, very limited rural presence. Large portions contested.

2023.assessment: Elections repeatedly postponed (scheduled 2022, pushed to 2024). Kiir extended transitional period. Inter-communal violence in Jonglei and other regions continued. Sudan war April 2023 created new pressure — hundreds of thousands of Sudanese refugees entered South Sudan. Oil revenues disrupted by Sudan war (pipeline through Sudan). Government continued to hold Juba and state capitals. Large portions of country remain outside effective control.

2024.assessment: Elections again postponed to December 2026. Tensions between Kiir and Machar renewed — Machar detained in April 2024 amid renewed political tensions. Upper Nile State fighting between factions. Inter-communal violence continued. Oil pipelines through Sudan affected by Sudan war. Sudan refugee influx continuing. Government authority limited outside Juba and capitals. Large portions contested.
