2025.assessment: As of March 2025, political tensions remain acute following Machar's detention. Inter-communal and factional violence continues. Sudan war continues to send refugees and disrupt oil exports. Government holds Juba and nominal state capitals. Large portions of the country remain outside effective central government control with multiple armed groups and communities operating autonomously.

sources.citation: UNMISS: South Sudan Situation Reports early 2025

2005.assessment: GOSS pre-independence. No formal budget published. CPA wealth-sharing formula provided oil revenues to GOSS but no public accounting. No budget documents publicly available. No information whatsoever.

2006.assessment: GOSS not yet producing formal budget documentation. Some internal government budget documents but not publicly accessible. No information.

2007.assessment: GOSS attempted some budget documentation under donor pressure. Very minimal — internal documents, not public. No information in meaningful sense.

2008.assessment: Some GOSS budget documents beginning to appear for donor accountability. Still not publicly accessible in meaningful sense. Scant — executive budget proposed but very limited access. OBS equivalent scant (<20).

sources.citation: IBP: Southern Sudan 2008 — donor accountability documents

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum. Minimal budget documentation. GOSS budget process developing but extremely opaque. Donor pressure for accountability. Scant — executive budget with very limited disclosure.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum elections required some budget accountability. Minimal improvement in documentation. Scant — executive budget, no public audit, no legislature oversight mechanism. Scant disclosure.

2011.assessment: Independence July 2011. OBS first covered South Sudan in 2012 survey (covering 2011). Score 5/100 — near bottom globally. Executive budget available but no pre-budget, audit, or mid-year reports public. Scant disclosure.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2012: South Sudan OBS score 5/100

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown devastated budget process. Government scrambling for revenue — even less transparency. OBS score 5/100. Scant — only executive budget existing, limited in practice.

2013.assessment: Oil resumed — some budget normalization. December 2013 civil war destroyed any budget accountability. Emergency spending. OBS approximately 5/100. Scant — limited disclosure before civil war.

2014.assessment: Civil war. Government spending on military operations — no budget published. Complete opacity. No information.

sources.citation: IBP: South Sudan 2014 — civil war no budget

2015.assessment: OBS 2015 score 0/100 for South Sudan — absolute bottom. Civil war, fiscal collapse, no budget published. No information.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2015: South Sudan — OBS 0/100

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse, fiscal collapse, hyperinflation. No budget published. No information.

2017.assessment: Famine, civil war, fiscal collapse. OBS 0/100. No budget published. No information.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2017: South Sudan — OBS 0/100

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Marginal improvement in budget documentation. OBS 4/100. Executive budget documents produced but extremely limited. Scant.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2019: South Sudan — OBS 4/100

2019.assessment: Pre-RTGoNU. R-ARCSS implementation. Budget transparency marginal. OBS ~4/100. Scant.

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed. COVID disruption. OBS 4/100 continuing. Scant — no meaningful improvement in budget disclosure.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2021: South Sudan — OBS 4/100

2021.assessment: Marginal. OBS 4/100. Legislative oversight mechanisms still absent in practice. Scant.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2021: South Sudan OBS 4/100

2022.assessment: Marginal improvement. High oil prices allowed some budget normalization. OBS 6/100. Still scant — no major transparency improvement.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey 2023: South Sudan OBS 6/100

2023.assessment: Sudan war disrupted oil exports. Fiscal crisis returning. Budget transparency not improving. OBS ~6/100. Scant.

2024.assessment: Sudan war, Machar detention, fiscal collapse. Budget transparency degraded from already minimal. No information on public spending.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, political instability, fiscal collapse. No budget transparency. No information.

2005.assessment: Pre-independence. GNPOC (Greater Nile Petroleum Operating Company) controlled oil — operated by CNPC, Petronas, ONGC with Sudan government. Complete opacity. CPA wealth-sharing formula transferred revenues to GOSS but no transparency on actual extraction figures. No disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI: Southern Sudan not member; GNPOC opacity

2006.assessment: GNPOC continuing. Complete oil sector opacity. No EITI. No disclosure.

2007.assessment: GNPOC. No disclosure. No EITI membership discussions. Complete opacity.

2008.assessment: Oil peak year. GOSS receiving transfers from Khartoum. GNPOC no disclosure. Donors occasionally raised transparency but GOSS not pursuing EITI. No disclosure.

2009.assessment: Post-GFC. No EITI. GNPOC opacity. No disclosure.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum. GOSS had some discussions with donors about post-independence extractive governance. No EITI candidacy. No disclosure.

2011.assessment: Independence July 2011. GOSS/Government engaged with EITI in discussions. Oil sector (Nilepet established as national oil company). Civil society and donors pressed for EITI candidacy. No formal EITI application filed. Minimal disclosure — government acknowledged interest without commitment.

sources.citation: EITI: South Sudan 2011 — discussions, no formal candidacy

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown January 2012. Any EITI candidacy discussions frozen by crisis. Government focused on oil dispute, not transparency. No disclosure. Complete opacity.

2013.assessment: Oil resumed. Civil society renewed EITI push. Government distracted by political crisis (Kiir dismissed cabinet July, civil war December). December 2013 civil war ended all EITI discussions. No disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI: South Sudan 2013 — civil war ended discussions

2014.assessment: Civil war. Oil sector funding war effort — no transparency. No EITI. No disclosure.

2015.assessment: Civil war, oil fields destroyed, ARCSS. No EITI discussions. No disclosure.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse. No EITI. Oil sector funding conflict. Complete opacity. No disclosure.

2017.assessment: Famine, war. No EITI. No disclosure.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Some civil society revived EITI discussion as part of peace dividend agenda. Government acknowledged extractive governance reforms needed. No formal EITI application — minimal disclosure discussions.

sources.citation: EITI: South Sudan 2018 — R-ARCSS peace governance discussions

2019.assessment: R-ARCSS pre-transition. Some Nilepet transparency discussions. Donors pushed for extractive sector reforms. No formal EITI. Minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI/donors: South Sudan 2019 extractive reform discussions

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed. COVID, oil crash. EITI discussions not progressed. No membership. Minimal disclosure.

2021.assessment: Elections postponed. EITI not progressed. No membership. Minimal disclosure.

2022.assessment: High oil prices. EITI discussions never formalized into candidacy. Government opaque on oil revenues. No membership. Minimal disclosure — government continued to resist.

2023.assessment: Sudan war disrupted oil. Pipeline closed. Any EITI discussions irrelevant as sector in crisis. No disclosure.

2024.assessment: Sudan war, Machar detained, fiscal collapse. No EITI. Oil sector completely opaque. No disclosure.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war ongoing, political instability. No EITI. Oil sector opaque. No disclosure.

2005.assessment: GOSS pre-independence. No formal legal code for southern Sudan. SPLM decree rule. Traditional/customary law operated in communities. No published laws, no formal courts (only military courts). No accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: ICG: Southern Sudan governance 2005 — SPLM decree rule

2006.assessment: SPLM governing by decree. Some GOSS legal framework drafting but not accessible. No published laws. No accessible framework.

2007.assessment: Some GOSS laws drafted and gazetted. Constitution of Southern Sudan 2005 provided formal framework but enforcement minimal. No accessible framework in practice.

sources.citation: GOSS: Transitional Constitution of Southern Sudan 2005 — limited implementation

2008.assessment: GOSS building legal framework under CPA. Some courts established in Juba. But rural areas under customary/military authority. Minimal formal legal framework — minimal rule by decree transitioning.

2009.assessment: Some legal institution building. GOSS judiciary developing. Corruption high. SPLA above the law. Minimal rule by decree.

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum. GOSS developing legal framework for independence. Some improvements in formal laws. Minimal rule by decree — some laws published, enforcement weak.

2011.assessment: Independence. Transitional Constitution adopted. Formal legal system established. Some optimism about rule of law. But capacity extremely weak. Corruption rampant. Minimal rule by decree — constitutional framework on paper.

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown. Legal institutions functioning minimally. SPLA impunity. NSS arrests without warrant. Courts functioning in Juba for commercial matters. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: HRW: South Sudan 2012 — SPLA impunity, NSS arrests

2013.assessment: Pre-civil war: minimal legal framework. December 2013 civil war — legal institutions collapsed. SPLA and SPLA-IO operated outside any law. No accessible framework during civil war.

sources.citation: HRW: South Sudan civil war 2013 — rule of law collapse

2014.assessment: Civil war. Courts non-functional outside Juba. SPLA/SPLA-IO committing atrocities with impunity. NSS operating death squads. No rule of law. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: HRW/Amnesty: South Sudan 2014 — war crimes, impunity

2015.assessment: WJP first rated South Sudan 2015: 0.29/1.00 (29/100) — near bottom globally. Civil war, Unity oil fields destroyed. Courts dysfunctional. No accessible framework in practice — formal score reflects severe deficiencies.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015: South Sudan — 0.29/1.00

2016.assessment: WJP 0.27/1.00 — worsening. ARCSS collapse July 2016. Legal institutions destroyed. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016: South Sudan — 0.27/1.00

2017.assessment: WJP ~0.26/1.00. Famine, war. Legal system essentially absent. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-18: South Sudan — ~0.26/1.00

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018. Some marginal legal improvement in Juba. WJP ~0.26/1.00. Minimal rule by decree — R-ARCSS framework creating some formal structures.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019: South Sudan — 0.26/1.00

2019.assessment: R-ARCSS implementation. WJP 0.26/1.00. Formal legal framework on paper, enforcement weak. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020: South Sudan — 0.26/1.00

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed. COVID disrupted courts. WJP 0.26/1.00. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021: South Sudan — 0.26/1.00

2021.assessment: Slight improvement in legal environment. WJP 0.27/1.00. Courts reopening in more areas. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022: South Sudan — 0.27/1.00

2022.assessment: WJP 0.27/1.00 approximately. Elections postponed. Legal reform stalled. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023: South Sudan — ~0.27/1.00

2023.assessment: Sudan war added instability. WJP declining slightly — approximately 0.26/1.00. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023: South Sudan — ~0.26/1.00

2024.assessment: Machar detained, Sudan war, fiscal collapse. Legal institutions functioning minimally. No accessible framework re-emerging.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Sudan war, Machar detained, political instability. Legal framework non-functional. No accessible framework.

2005.assessment: GOSS pre-independence. Southern Sudan was part of Sudan which consistently ranked in bottom 10% for press freedom. SPLM controlled information. Journalists faced harassment. Very serious situation.

2006.assessment: GOSS controlled information tightly. Some independent radio and print media in Juba but facing intimidation. Very serious.

2007.assessment: Some independent media growing in Juba. GOSS attempted some media liberalization for international image. Still severe restrictions — journalists covering sensitive topics harassed. Very serious.

2008.assessment: Media environment slightly improving pre-referendum. Some independent newspapers, radio. But SPLA-linked intimidation ongoing. Self-censorship high. Very serious.

2009.assessment: Pre-referendum period. International attention brought slightly improved environment. Journalists covering CPA-related issues had more space. Very serious but marginally better.

2010.assessment: Elections monitored internationally — some media space for election reporting. GOSS showed some tolerance under international attention. Still severe restrictions on critical coverage. Very serious.

2011.assessment: Independence brought initial press freedom optimism. RSF ranked South Sudan 111/179 — middle of global rankings, meaning press conditions were difficult but not among worst. New media outlets launched. Some independent reporting allowed. Difficult environment.

sources.citation: RSF: South Sudan 2011 — 111/179 World Press Freedom Index

2012.assessment: Deteriorating from independence optimism. Oil shutdown tensions led to journalist arrests. SPLA interfered with coverage. RSF 124/179. Very serious situation.

2013.assessment: Pre-civil war declining press freedom. December 2013 civil war — journalists expelled immediately. Media outlets closed. SPLA targeted journalists. Press freedom collapsed. No press freedom by year end.

sources.citation: RSF/CPJ: South Sudan 2013 — civil war December

2014.assessment: Civil war. CPJ documented multiple journalists killed and imprisoned. Government propaganda only. Journalists face death threats. No press freedom in practice.

2015.assessment: Civil war continuing. RSF 140/180. Journalists imprisoned, killed, expelled. Newspapers and radio stations closed by military. CPJ named South Sudan one of world's most dangerous. No press freedom.

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse July 2016. SPLA attacked journalists at Terrain Hotel. International journalists fled. Foreign journalists banned. RSF ~140/180. No press freedom.

sources.citation: CPJ: South Sudan 2016 — Terrain Hotel attack

2017.assessment: Famine. RSF 143/180. Journalists killed documenting famine. NSS arrested journalists. Self-censorship total. Radio stations closed. No press freedom.

2018.assessment: R-ARCSS September 2018 — marginal improvement in second half. RSF 149/180. Still near bottom. NSS continuing arrests. Self-censorship extreme. Very serious.

2019.assessment: Some improvement post-R-ARCSS. Some media outlets reopened. RSF 139/180. Still very serious restrictions, journalist imprisonment continues.

2020.assessment: RTGoNU formed. COVID restrictions added layer of censorship. RSF 139/180. Very serious.

2021.assessment: Slight worsening. NSS continuing journalist arrests. Elections postponed. RSF 145/180. Very serious.

2022.assessment: Marginal improvement. High oil revenues slightly eased economic pressure on media. RSF 137/180. Still very serious.

2023.assessment: Sudan war added complications. Journalists covering Sudanese refugee crisis harassed. RSF ~137/180. Very serious.

2024.assessment: Machar detained — journalists covering political situation face extreme danger. Sudan war continuing. RSF ~140/180. Very serious to no press freedom on political issues.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Political instability, Machar detained. Journalism remains extremely dangerous. Very serious.

2005.assessment: GOSS pre-independence. No national statistical office. GOSS relied on Sudan central statistics for any data. No independent data collection. No official statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank: Southern Sudan 2005 — no statistical capacity

2006.assessment: No statistical capacity. Some donor-funded surveys beginning (UNICEF MICS). GOSS no national statistics bureau. No official statistics.

2007.assessment: Some household surveys conducted by donors (WFP, UNICEF). No official statistical bureau. Donor-dependent data only. Extremely limited.

2008.assessment: Southern Sudan Bureau of Statistics established in Juba with donor support. Very nascent — minimal capacity. Extremely limited.

sources.citation: GOSS: Southern Sudan Bureau of Statistics established 2008

2009.assessment: Bureau of Statistics beginning some data collection. Household survey (SSHS) conducted with World Bank support. Nascent but existing capacity. Extremely limited but improving.

sources.citation: World Bank: Southern Sudan Household Survey 2009

2010.assessment: National population census planned but delayed to after referendum. Some capacity building. Bureau of Statistics operational with donor support. Extremely limited.

2011.assessment: Independence. National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) established. World Bank statistical capacity score approximately 21/100 — near bottom globally. Census planned for 2014 with UNFPA support. Extremely limited but building.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Statistical Capacity 2011 — ~21/100

2012.assessment: Oil shutdown devastated government resources for statistics. NBS underfunded. Statistical capacity stagnant. Extremely limited.

sources.citation: World Bank: South Sudan Statistical Capacity 2012 — 21/100

2016.assessment: ARCSS collapse, fiscal collapse. NBS destroyed. Statistical capacity near zero. No official statistics.

2021.assessment: Some improvement in statistical infrastructure. NBS continuing. Oil data improving. But census still not held. Extremely limited improving.

2024.assessment: Fiscal collapse, Machar detained, Sudan war. Statistical capacity further degraded. Extremely limited to no official statistics.
